Definition of Terms, Acronyms, and Abbreviations
AI Model,Machine learning model for ingredient recognition using TensorFlow.js
Recipe,Structured cooking instructions with ingredients and steps
Ingredient Recognition,AI process identifying food items from images
Dietary Preference,User food restrictions (vegetarian, gluten-free, etc.)
SSL/TLS,Secure Socket Layer / Transport Layer Security

This Software Design Specification (SDS) document describes the complete architectural and detailed design for the Chef-Mate: AI-Driven Recipe Companion system. The document serves as a blueprint for the development team, providing comprehensive technical specifications including system architecture, component designs, database schemas, API structures, and interface definitions.

The design follows Object-Oriented Design (OOD) methodology with a three-tier architecture pattern (Presentation, Business Logic, and Data Access layers). This document translates the requirements specified in SRS document (S22F25-SRS v2.0) into a technical implementation roadmap.
- Development Team: Software engineers implementing the system components
- Database Administrators: Personnel responsible for database design and optimization
- Quality Assurance Team: Testers who will validate the system against design specifications
- Project Managers: Stakeholders monitoring development progress
- System Architects: Technical leads reviewing architectural decisions
- Maintenance Team: Future developers who will maintain and enhance the system
This document uses the following conventions:
- Font: Arial 11pt for body text, Arial 14pt Bold for section headers
- Code Samples: Courier New 10pt for code snippets and technical identifiers
- Diagrams: UML 2.5 notation for all architectural and design diagrams
- Tables: Grid format with header rows in bold
- File Paths: Italic text for directory and file references
- Requirement References: Format [REQ-ID] linking to SRS document

Chef-Mate is a web-based AI-powered recipe recommendation and management system designed to help home cooks optimize ingredient usage and reduce food waste. The system leverages artificial intelligence for ingredient recognition from images and provides personalized recipe recommendations based on available ingredients and dietary preferences.
- Frontend: React 18 with Vite build tool and responsive design
- Backend: Node.js with Express.js RESTful API server
- Authentication: JWT-based token authentication with bcrypt password hashing
- AI Integration: TensorFlow.js for client-side ingredient recognition
- Deployment: Cloud-based hosting with HTTPS/SSL encryption
The system architecture follows a client-server model with RESTful API communication, implementing industry-standard security practices and scalable design patterns.
- Complete system architecture including frontend, backend, and database layers
- Detailed component designs for user authentication, recipe management, and AI recognition
- Database schema with tables for users, recipes, favorites, likes, and comments
- RESTful API endpoint specifications with request/response formats
- Security mechanisms including JWT authentication, password encryption, and input validation
- User interface design patterns and responsive layout strategies
- AI module integration for ingredient recognition
- Online grocery shopping functionality (not in project scope)
- Users have modern web browsers (Chrome 90+, Firefox 88+, Safari 14+)
- Minimum 5 Mbps internet connection available to users
- Client devices have 5MP+ cameras for image capture
- Server environment supports Node.js v18+ and MySQL 8.0+
- SSL/TLS certificates available for HTTPS communication
- MySQL Database Server: Required for data persistence and relational data management
- Node.js Runtime: Required for server-side JavaScript execution
- TensorFlow.js Library: Required for AI-based ingredient recognition
- Cloud Storage Service: Required for recipe image hosting (e.g., AWS S3, Cloudinary)
- JWT Library: jsonwebtoken npm package for token generation/validation
- bcryptjs Library: For secure password hashing
- Express.js Framework: Web application framework for Node.js
- Vite Build Tool: Frontend development and build tooling
- Database schema must be created before API endpoints can function
- Authentication middleware must be implemented before protected routes
- User registration must complete before recipe creation is available
- Frontend build process depends on successful API integration
- Risk: TensorFlow.js model may not accurately recognize all ingredients
- Mitigation: Implement fallback manual ingredient entry; continuous model training
- Contingency: Allow users to correct AI predictions and use feedback for improvement
- Risk: MySQL may experience performance degradation with 500+ concurrent users
- Mitigation: Implement connection pooling, query optimization, and database indexing
- Contingency: Horizontal scaling with read replicas; consider caching layer (Redis)
- Risk: Token theft or unauthorized access if secrets are compromised
- Mitigation: Use strong JWT secrets, implement token expiration and refresh mechanisms
- Contingency: Token blacklisting system; immediate secret rotation capability
- Risk: Large volume of recipe images may exceed storage capacity
- Mitigation: Use cloud storage with auto-scaling; implement image compression
- Contingency: Image size limits; optional image hosting
- Risk: External services may have downtime or rate limits
- Mitigation: Implement retry logic and graceful degradation
- Contingency: Queue system for delayed processing; fallback services
- AI model architecture may require updates based on accuracy testing
- Recipe recommendation algorithm may need tuning based on user feedback
- UI/UX design may evolve based on usability testing results
- Database schema may require optimization after performance testing
- API endpoint structures may change during integration testing
Chef-Mate implements a Three-Tier Client-Server Architecture:
- Responsibility: User interface rendering, user input handling, client-side validation
- Components: React components, routing, state management, HTTP client
- Communication: REST API calls over HTTPS to backend server
- Responsibility: Business logic processing, authentication, API management
- Components: Express routes, middleware, controllers, authentication services
- Communication: SQL queries to database, JSON responses to frontend
- Responsibility: Data persistence, relational data management, query processing
- Components: Database tables, indexes, foreign key relationships
- Communication: SQL protocol over TCP port 3306
- Components: Login, Registration, JWT Token Management
- Components: Recipe CRUD operations, Search, Filter
- Components: Image processing, TensorFlow.js model, Ingredient detection
- Components: Profile management, Dietary preferences, User settings
- Cloud Storage API: For recipe image uploads/downloads
- SMTP Server: For email notifications (future enhancement)
- Monitoring Service: For system health and performance metrics
- All API endpoints return consistent error response format: {error: string, details: string}
- HTTP status codes follow REST conventions (200, 201, 400, 401, 404, 500)
- Backend logs all errors with timestamps and stack traces
- JWT tokens required for protected endpoints
- SQL injection prevention through parameterized queries
- CORS middleware configured for allowed origins
- Input validation on all user-submitted data
│   ├── Recipe Controller (create, read, update, delete, search)
1. User interacts with UI (e.g., clicks 'Login' button)
2. React component captures event and calls API service function
3. API service sends HTTP POST request to /api/auth/login with credentials
4. Express server receives request at auth route
6. Auth controller queries database for user
10. Response sent back to frontend with token and user data
12. UI updates to show authenticated state
1. User requests protected resource (e.g., create recipe)
2. Frontend includes JWT token in Authorization header
4. If valid, request proceeds to controller
5. Controller processes business logic and database operations
- App.js: Root component with routing configuration
- RecipeContext.js: Global recipe data state provider
- LoginPage.js: User login form and authentication
- HomePage.js: Recipe browsing and search interface
- RecipeDetailPage.js: Detailed recipe view with interactions
- CreateRecipePage.js: Recipe creation form with image upload
- ProfilePage.js: User profile and preferences management
- RecipeCard.js: Recipe preview card for lists
- SearchBar.js: Recipe search input with filters
- Input.js: Styled input component with validation
- server/index.js: Express app initialization and server startup
- server/config/database.js: MySQL connection pool and initialization
- server/routes/auth.js: Authentication endpoints (register, login, logout, verify)
- recipes: Recipe data with JSON fields for ingredients/instructions
The system is designed with extensibility in mind:
- Independent route modules allow adding new features without modifying existing code
- Example: Adding /api/meal-plan route for meal planning feature
- All API endpoints can be versioned (e.g., /api/v1/recipes, /api/v2/recipes)
- Allows backward compatibility when introducing breaking changes
- JSON columns in recipes table allow flexible data without schema changes
- New tables can be added without affecting existing functionality
- Foreign key relationships support future linked features
- AI recognition module designed as separate service
- Can swap or upgrade AI models without backend changes
- Supports multiple AI providers (TensorFlow.js, external APIs)
- Meal planning feature with weekly calendar
- Advanced recipe recommendations using collaborative filtering
- Mobile application using same backend API
- JWT authentication middleware reusable across all protected routes
- Password hashing utility functions can be used for admin accounts
- Exported pool object used by all database operations
- Button, Input, Modal components reused throughout application
- RecipeCard component used in multiple views (home, favorites, search results)
- Frontend API client functions reusable across components
- Centralized HTTP configuration and error handling
- Middleware Pattern: Authentication, logging, error handling
- Higher-Order Components: Authentication wrappers for React components
- Service Layer Pattern: Business logic separated from route handlers
- Repository Pattern: Database access abstracted into reusable functions
- Flexible grid layouts using CSS Flexbox and Grid
- Complex forms broken into steps (recipe creation wizard)
- Advanced search options hidden behind expandable panels
- Recipe details revealed on demand (not all visible initially)
- Optimistic UI updates (like button immediately reflects action)
- Fixed header with main navigation always visible
- Keyboard navigation support (tab order, focus states)
- Color Palette: Primary (chef orange), Secondary (food green), Neutral (grays)
- Typography: Sans-serif for UI (Arial), Serif for recipe titles (Georgia)
- Spacing: 8px base unit for consistent margins and padding
- Elevation: Shadow levels for cards and modals
- User accounts, recipes, interactions stored in normalized tables
- Referential integrity enforced through foreign keys
- Recipe ingredients stored as JSON array in recipes.ingredients
- Recipe instructions stored as JSON array in recipes.instructions
- Allows flexible schema for varying recipe structures
- Recipe images stored in external cloud storage (AWS S3/Cloudinary)
- Database stores only image URLs, not binary data
- Reduces database size and improves query performance
- Complex operations wrapped in transactions (e.g., recipe creation with tags)
- Rollback on errors to maintain data integrity
- Daily incremental backups (implemented at database level)
- User authentication state cached in localStorage
- Recently viewed recipes cached in sessionStorage
- Redis cache for frequently accessed recipes
- Each request is independent and self-contained
- No server-side session state (JWT tokens contain all auth info)
- Enables horizontal scaling without session synchronization
- MySQL InnoDB row-level locking prevents concurrent update conflicts
- Unique constraints prevent duplicate entries (user favorites, likes)
- Database queries don't block other requests
- AI image processing can be offloaded to worker processes (future)
- Prevents duplicate likes/favorites from simultaneous clicks
- Multiple identical requests produce same result
- Recipe creation is atomic (all or nothing)
- Comment posting includes validation before insert
- React Context API manages global state
- State updates trigger re-renders for UI consistency
- WebSocket support for live comment updates
- Like counts may briefly lag during high concurrency
- ProtectedRoute.jsx: Route guard for authenticated pages
- RecipeList.jsx: Grid/list view of recipes with pagination
- RecipeCard.jsx: Individual recipe preview with image, title, cuisine, difficulty
- RecipeDetail.jsx: Full recipe view with ingredients, instructions, comments
- RecipeForm.jsx: Create/edit recipe form with dynamic ingredient/instruction lists
- LikeButton.jsx: Like/dislike toggle with count display
- CommentSection.jsx: Comment list and input form
- IngredientRecognition.jsx: Display AI-detected ingredients with editing
│   └── database.js - MySQL connection pool and table initialization
- Middleware stack: CORS, JSON parser, error handler
- initializeDatabase() creates tables if not exist
- Exported pool for use in route handlers
- authenticateToken: Verifies JWT token, attaches user to req.user
- optionalAuth: Allows requests with or without token (for public/private content)
* Process: Hash password with bcrypt (12 salt rounds)
* Input: JWT token in Authorization header
* Process: SQL query with WHERE conditions and JSON parsing
* Input: {title, description, ingredients[], instructions[], cuisine, prep_time, cook_time, servings, difficulty, image_url}
* Process: Insert with JSON.stringify for array fields
* Process: Update with JSON.stringify for array fields
* Process: Cascading delete (removes likes, comments, favorites)
* Process: INSERT ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE
- All errors returned in format: {error: string, details?: string}
- HTTP status codes: 200 (success), 201 (created), 400 (bad request), 401 (unauthorized), 404 (not found), 409 (conflict), 500 (server error)
- Console logging for all errors with stack traces
- Graceful error messages to prevent information leakage
- JWT secret stored in environment variable (process.env.JWT_SECRET)
- Parameterized SQL queries prevent SQL injection
Connection: mysql2 driver with connection pooling
Charset: utf8mb4 (supports emoji and international characters)
(See Section 6 for detailed database schema and data dictionary)
Authentication: Bearer token in Authorization header
- search: string (optional) - Search in title, description, ingredients
- cuisine: string (optional) - Filter by cuisine type
- difficulty: enum (optional) - Filter by difficulty (easy, medium, hard)
Response (200): Single recipe object with interactions
Request: Same as POST (partial updates supported)
Model: Pre-trained image classification model (MobileNet or custom trained model)
Deployment: Client-side (in browser) or Server-side (Python Flask API)
- Resize image to model input size (e.g., 224x224)
- Pass preprocessed image tensor to model
- Get prediction probabilities for ingredient classes
- Map class indices to ingredient names
- Return sorted list of detected ingredients
- Image too large: 413 Payload Too Large
- Compress images before sending to model
- Use Web Workers for non-blocking processing (client-side)
- Cache model in memory (don't reload on each request)
- One user can create many recipes
- Each recipe is created by one user
- ON DELETE CASCADE: Deleting user deletes all their recipes
2. users (1) ---- likes ----> (M) recipe_likes (M) <---- belongs to (1) recipes
- Many-to-Many relationship through recipe_likes junction table
- One user can like many recipes
- One recipe can be liked by many users
- Foreign Keys: recipe_likes.user_id -> users.id, recipe_likes.recipe_id -> recipes.id
- Many-to-Many relationship through recipe_comments junction table
- One user can write many comments
- One recipe can have many comments
- Foreign Keys: recipe_comments.user_id -> users.id, recipe_comments.recipe_id -> recipes.id
4. users (1) ---- saves ----> (M) user_favorites (M) <---- marked as (1) recipes
- Many-to-Many relationship through user_favorites junction table
- One user can favorite many recipes
- One recipe can be favorited by many users
- Foreign Keys: user_favorites.user_id -> users.id, user_favorites.recipe_id -> recipes.id
Where-used: Authentication system, recipe creation, comments, likes, favorites
Description: Stores user account information for authentication and profile management
- CHECK: email must contain @ symbol (application layer validation)
- CHECK: password length >= 60 characters (bcrypt hash length)
- UNIQUE index on email (for login queries)
Where-used: Recipe browsing, search, detail view, user created recipes
Description: Stores recipe information including ingredients, instructions, and metadata
- FOREIGN KEY (created_by) REFERENCES users(id) ON DELETE CASCADE
- INDEX idx_created_by on created_by (for user's recipes)
["2 cups flour", "1 tsp salt", "3 eggs", "1 cup milk"]
["Mix dry ingredients", "Beat eggs with milk", "Combine and stir", "Cook for 20 minutes"]
Where-used: Like/dislike functionality, recipe popularity calculation
Description: Junction table storing user likes and dislikes for recipes
updated_at,When like was last changed,TIMESTAMP,-,No,CURRENT_TIMESTAMP ON UPDATE,-
- FOREIGN KEY (user_id) REFERENCES users(id) ON DELETE CASCADE
- FOREIGN KEY (recipe_id) REFERENCES recipes(id) ON DELETE CASCADE
- UNIQUE index on (user_id, recipe_id) - prevents duplicate likes
- INDEX on recipe_id (for counting recipe likes)
- User can only have one like/dislike per recipe (enforced by unique constraint)
- Toggling like to dislike updates existing record (INSERT ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE)
- Deleting user cascades to remove all their likes
- Deleting recipe cascades to remove all likes on that recipe
Where-used: Recipe detail page comment section
Description: Stores user comments on recipes
updated_at,When comment was last edited,TIMESTAMP,-,No,CURRENT_TIMESTAMP ON UPDATE,-
- FOREIGN KEY (recipe_id) REFERENCES recipes(id) ON DELETE CASCADE
- FOREIGN KEY (user_id) REFERENCES users(id) ON DELETE CASCADE
- CHECK: content length > 0 (enforced at application layer)
- INDEX on recipe_id (for fetching recipe comments)
- INDEX on user_id (for fetching user's comments)
- Comments retrieved ordered by created_at DESC (newest first)
- No unique constraint (user can comment multiple times)
- Deleting recipe cascades to remove all comments
- Deleting user cascades to remove all their comments
Where-used: User favorites page, favorite button functionality
Description: Junction table storing recipes favorited/saved by users
- FOREIGN KEY (user_id) REFERENCES users(id) ON DELETE CASCADE
- FOREIGN KEY (recipe_id) REFERENCES recipes(id) ON DELETE CASCADE
- UNIQUE index on (user_id, recipe_id) - prevents duplicate favorites
- INDEX on user_id (for fetching user's favorites)
- User can favorite each recipe only once (enforced by unique constraint)
- Deleting user cascades to remove all their favorites
- Deleting recipe cascades to remove all favorites of that recipe
- Favorites retrieved ordered by created_at DESC (most recent first)
